query: embryological development of upper lip formation

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Facial Primordia
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary processes expand midline to merge with the medial nasal processes. Fusion with the medial nasal processes results in mesenchymal commingling in the intermaxillary territory, producing a single, continuous maxilla and the upper lip and palate. The premaxilla and gingival lining, primary palate, and deep median portion of the labium superiorus (upper lip) arise from the intermaxillary segment. The lateral portion of the labium superiorus, most of the maxilla, and the secondary palate derive from the maxillary processes. The maxillary process outgrowths cover the inferior aspect of the medial nasal processes to form the philtrum of the upper lip. An orofacial cleft occurs when the processes fail to fuse properly.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: By the third week of gestation, the oropharyngeal membrane becomes apparent as the primordial oral orifice. Surrounding this membrane, the external face is formed by the development of the neural crest-derived frontonasal process covering the forebrain and the first pharyngeal arch composed of mesoderm and neural crest tissues. Further differentiation of the frontonasal prominences gives rise to a pair of lateral nasal processes and a pair of medial nasal processes that eventually give rise to the globular process of the philtrum. The first pharyngeal arch develops into a pair of mandibular processes and a pair of maxillary processes that derive from outgrowths of the arch. The developmental origins of facial structures are outlined in Table 1. The centripetal growth of these prominences results in fusion by the thirteenth week of gestation. Failure of the formation or union of any of these processes may give rise to a developmental defect known as a facial cleft that occurs at a rate of approximately one per 700 births. Clefts may be limited to the lip or extend into the hard palate. Etiologic factors responsible for facial clefts include genetic predisposition, in utero teratogen exposure (antiepileptics, methotrexate, isotretinoin) or maternal viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Cavity (Mouth)  -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The upper lip form during early embryogenesis from the maxillary, lateral nasal, and medial nasal facial prominences. These facial prominences develop from anteriorly migrating neural crest cells in combination with mesoderm and the head ectoderm. They then undergo growth and expansion, eventually leading to their fusion and the formation of facial structures. [4] The mandibular processes fuse to form the lower lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the development of the fetus, the external human face starts to develop during the fourth through sixth weeks. The face derives from the first two pharyngeal arches, neural crest cells, frontonasal prominence, medial nasal prominence, oropharyngeal membrane, and lateral nasal prominence. During week four of development, the oropharyngeal membrane breaks down to create the oral cavity. The frontonasal prominences develop into the forehead, bridge of the nose, medial nasal prominences, and lateral nasal prominences. The medial nasal prominences will further develop into the primary palate, philtrum, upper four incisors, and parts of the jaw. The lateral nasal prominences develop into the sides of the nose. The first pharyngeal arch will form the cheeks, lateral upper lip, lateral upper jaw, and secondary palate. The second pharyngeal arch will form the lower lip and jaw. All these structures form bilaterally and migrate toward the midline before fusing. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Craniofacial Growth, And Development -- Development -- Face
retrieved_docs.content: In the fifth week, ring-like bilateral thickening develops on the frontonasal prominence, called nasal placodes. The edge of the placode enlarges, whereas the middle becomes pitted to form nasal pits. The lateral nasal prominence forms from the lateral edge of the placode, and the medial nasal prominence from the medial edge. Over the next two weeks, the maxillary processes grow medially to the lateral nasal prominence until they fuse. The deep groove between the maxillary processes and the lateral nasal prominence deepens and forms a tube - the nasolacrimal duct. This growth continues compressing the medial nasal prominences towards the midline until they touch and fuse, forming the middle of the nose and the philtrum of the lip. The mandibular processes fuse in the midline.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip Repair -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In order to appreciate the anatomy of cleft lip deformity, it is vital to have a thorough understanding of the embryology behind this disorder. The first pharyngeal arch begins to develop facial prominences around the fourth week of embryonic development. These structures are the maxillary prominences, derived from neural crest cells, and the nasal placodes, derived from surface ectoderm. Around the fifth week of embryonic development, the nasal placodes invaginate, forming the nasal pits, creating a ridge of tissue around the pits called the lateral nasal prominence at the lateral aspect and the medial nasal prominence at the medial aspect. During the sixth to seventh weeks of embryonic development, the bilateral maxillary prominences grow medially towards the medial nasal prominences, and fusion occurs, leading to the formation of the upper lip. The fusion of the bilateral medial nasal prominences forms the medial portion of the upper lip, whereas the paired maxillary prominences form the lateral aspects of the upper lip. Further medial growth of the maxillary prominences leads to additional fusion of the deep medial nasal prominences, which creates the primary palate. The primary palate includes the four incisors and the palate anterior to the incisive foramen. Additionally, the medial nasal prominences form the philtrum, the columella, and the nasal tip. The lateral nasal prominences are involved in the development of the nose and do not contribute to upper lip formation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Etiology -- Abnormal Embryological Development of the Palate
retrieved_docs.content: By week 5 of embryogenesis, the frontonasal prominence develops into medial and lateral nasal processes via a cleavage. These medial and lateral nasal processes then fuse with the maxillary process to form the upper lip. The medial nasal processes then fuse in the midline to form the intermaxillary segment, which forms the philtrum of the upper lip and the primary palate (ie, the portion of the palate anterior to the incisive foramen) in gestational week 6. [4] The lateral nasal processes will go on to form the nasal alae. The mandibular processes fuse in the midline to form the lower lip and mandible, while the lateral fusion of the mandibular and maxillary processes forms the oral commissures. Failure, disruption, or interruption of any of these fusion points can result in clefting.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillofacial development begins in the 4th week of gestation, with the formation of 5 facial prominences around the stomodeum, the primordial mouth and topographical center of the face. [15] Contributions from the 1st pharyngeal arch and neural crest cells give rise to the 5 prominences: paired maxillary, paired mandibular, and the frontonasal prominence. [15] [16] [17] The stomodeum is bordered cranially by the frontonasal prominence, laterally by the maxillary prominence, and inferolaterally by the mandibular prominence. The maxillary prominences develop into the secondary palate, most of the maxilla, and the lateral upper lip (see Image . Anterior Surface of the Maxilla at Birth). [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Facial Primordia
retrieved_docs.content: Other relevant events happen during this period of facial development. In the 5th week, the oropharyngeal membrane degenerates, producing an opening between the cranial foregut and the extraembryonic environment. Myoblasts from the 1st pair of pharyngeal arches produce the mastication muscles, while myoblasts from the 2nd pair of pharyngeal arches produce the facial expression muscles. The eyes begin to move ventrally from their initial lateral positions as the rest of the head grows and develops. The external ears appear in the cervical region and then rise to the head's lateral aspect. By the end of developmental week 7, the embryo has facial features with a typical human-like appearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Palate
retrieved_docs.content: The palate lies between the nasal and oral cavities and arises from the primary and secondary palates. By the 6th week, the intermaxillary segment develops from the fusion of the paired medial nasal processes and maxillary processes. This fusion gives rise to the primary palate. The oronasal membrane develops posteriorly as the barrier between the nasal and oral epithelia becomes thinner. [3] The membrane later ruptures and forms the primitive choanae that connect the oral cavity to the nasal cavity. The primary palate will give rise to the premaxilla, a triangular region occupying the anterior one-third of the palate. The premaxilla bears the incisive foramen and 4 upper incisors.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Facial Primordia
retrieved_docs.content: The paired maxillary processes grow larger medially toward the nasal and contralateral maxillary processes. The maxillary processes then fuse with the lateral nasal processes to form the nasolacrimal groove, the site where the cheek and lateral nasal territory merge. A rodlike ectodermal development forms at the nasolacrimal groove's base, producing an epithelial cord that canalizes to form both the nasolacrimal duct and lacrimal sac.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Palate
retrieved_docs.content: The secondary palate forms the rest of the hard palate and all of the soft palate. This region develops during the 7th and 8th weeks post-fertilization. The secondary palate forms from 2 palatal shelves—the lateral palatal processes—that are medial outgrowths of the maxillary processes. These processes grow inferiorly and parallel to the tongue. By the 8th week's end, the lateral palatal processes fuse and, with the primary palate, form the definitive palate. During this time, the nasal septum grows, separating the left and right nasal passages. The septum's inferior portion will merge with the definitive palate. Disruption of these developmental processes may result in the formation of a cleft palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Normal facial development enables individuals to perform critical auditory, visual, breathing, masticatory, expressive, and vocal functions. Facial primordia appears near the mouth's future site in the 4th week of development. Henceforth, facial development becomes a complex process involving the formation of various primordial tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the first eight weeks of gestation, the mesenchyme condenses and forms five prominences, two mandibular, two maxillary and a frontonasal prominence. The frontonasal prominence forms the precartilagenous nasal capsule which develops as two masses around the forming nasal cavities. The maxillary prominences grow and fuse at the midline forming the lateral nasal prominences.  As the frontonasal prominence regresses and the maxillary prominence grows medially, they form the midline of the nose and philtrum of the upper lip. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Pharyngeal Apparatus
retrieved_docs.content: The pharyngeal or branchial apparatus first appears during the 4th week of development and is critical in forming head and neck structures. The pharyngeal apparatus includes the pharyngeal arches, grooves or clefts, and pouches, all of which are comprised of mesenchymal tissue lined externally by ectoderm and internally by endoderm.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Development -- Facial Primordia
retrieved_docs.content: Frontonasal process - arises from mesenchyme produced by the neural crest cells near the forebrain and ventral midbrain. The frontal region of the frontonasal process produces the forehead, while the nasal portion produces the nose. Maxillary processes - paired structures arising from the 1st pharyngeal arches superior to the mandibular processes. Mesoderm and neural crest cells migrate into the 1st pharyngeal arches to generate maxillary mesenchyme during the 4th week of development. The maxillary processes will later produce a pair of palatal processes. the maxilla or upper jaw, the zygoma or cheekbone, and the squamous portion of the temporal bone. Mandibular processes - paired structures derived from the 1st pharyngeal arches. The 1st pharyngeal arches are also known as the mandibular arches. These processes will produce the mandible or lower jaw. These primordia fuse to form the face.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that is linked hierarchically to the more general term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the former is a child concept of the latter. This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the abdominal cavity (břišní dutina) is recognized as an allowable qualifier within the field of embryology, indicating that references to the abdominal cavity can be appropriately qualified by embryological context. Conversely, embryology itself serves as an allowable qualifier for specific craniofacial conditions, including the Arabic‑language terms فالج (cleft lip) and ورم الشفة (lip tumor), meaning that descriptions of these conditions may be properly qualified by their embryological origins or developmental aspects. This bidirectional qualification underscores the importance of precise anatomical and developmental descriptors in accurately characterizing both normal embryologic structures and related congenital or neoplastic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 963
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is an instance of the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that this fetal presentation can occur regardless of the specific clinical episode. The “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” category is classified as a type of “Malposition; malpresentation,” placing it within the overall group of abnormal fetal positions. Within the same “Malposition; malpresentation” group, other specific abnormalities are also classified, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The specific entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is linked to the Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” confirming that it refers to a fetal arm that has prolapsed. Additionally, “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is an instance of the broader “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” further illustrating the hierarchical organization of these malpresentation categories. Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy in which face or brow presentations, prolapsed fetal arms, and transverse or oblique presentations are all subtypes of malposition or malpresentation, each linked to more general descriptive categories that are independent of specific episodes of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 5 -- Community 2954
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” designates embryology as a relevant descriptor for thyroid disorders, retinal detachment (تمزق الشبكية), and lower‑extremity ulcers (قرحات الطرفين السفليين). Recognizing embryology as an appropriate qualifier highlights the importance of developmental origins in understanding and managing these diverse conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category of fetal malposition. Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which specific abnormal fetal presentations are organized under generic presentation terms, which in turn are subsumed by the overarching classification of malposition; malpresentation, a key concept for understanding and managing unstable fetal positions during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 731

Finally, Mentum presentation of fetus is identified as an isa relationship with مّجيء وَجْهي (face presentation), specifying that a mentum (chin) presentation is a particular form of facial fetal presentation. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how various fetal positions, malpresentations, and physiological reproductive events are organized and interpreted to guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: The fetal presentation entities “Face OR brow presentation of fetus” and “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” are each linked to more generic descriptors—“Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” respectively—indicating that the specific presentations are sub‑categories of broader, episode‑agnostic classifications. Both of these broader presentation categories are classified under the overarching obstetric condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which groups together abnormal fetal positions that deviate from the normal cephalic presentation. “Malposition; malpresentation” itself is identified as an indication for obstetric management within the domain “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” highlighting that any malpresentation warrants specific clinical attention during prenatal care, labor, and delivery planning. Within the “Malposition; malpresentation” group, two additional sub‑types are recognized: “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The prolapsed‑arm condition also has a parent term expressed in Arabic, “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” which serves as the linguistic equivalent of the English descriptor. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that specific fetal malpresentations—face or brow presentation, transverse or oblique presentation, and prolapsed fetal arm—are organized under the general category of malposition/malpresentation, which is a key indication for specialized obstetric care throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This structured classification aids clinicians in recognizing, documenting, and managing abnormal fetal positions to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2344
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific type of fetal presentation that is categorized under the broader term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the presentation is recognized without reference to a particular episode of care. This presentation is linked to a parent classification within obstetric terminology, reflecting its hierarchical relationship to more general categories of fetal presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.
